metadata.summary: Choose and implement the right API gateway pattern (simple routing, BFF, aggregation, edge+mesh) and select the right gateway technology for the system context.

content.title: API Gateway Patterns and Technology Selection
content.summary: An API gateway is a single entry point for all client requests to backend services, acting as a reverse proxy for cross-cutting concerns. Pick the right pattern (simple routing, BFF, aggregation, or edge+mesh) based on client diversity and backend topology, then select the right technology from Kong, AWS API Gateway, Traefik, Envoy, APISIX, Apigee, or Apollo Router.

why.p: API gateways decouple clients from backend service topology, centralize cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, observability), enable independent service evolution, and simplify client development. Without a gateway, every client must know every service's address, handle auth separately, and negotiate failures independently — making backend refactoring impossible without client updates.

Pattern choice is architectural: BFF gives each client type its own optimized surface; aggregation reduces round trips by composing responses; edge+mesh separates north-south traffic (gateway) from east-west traffic (service mesh). Picking the wrong pattern adds operational complexity without benefit.

list.item: Designing the front door for a new microservices or serverless system.
Multiple client types (web, mobile, IoT, partner APIs) with different data and auth needs.
Centralizing auth, rate limiting, routing, or observability across services.
Migrating from monolith to microservices and needing a stable external surface.
Selecting between gateway technologies given infra, team, and performance constraints.
Single monolith with one host — a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) is sufficient; gateway adds latency and ops cost without benefit.
Internal east-west traffic between services on a service mesh — that is mesh territory (Istio/Linkerd), not edge gateway.
Simple Lambda + Function URL deployments where API Gateway adds no value over direct invocation.
Real-time bidirectional streams that need persistent connections at scale — use AppSync, a dedicated WS gateway, or NATS/Centrifugo instead.

section.name: what-is-an-api-gateway
section.p: An API Gateway is a single entry point for all client requests to backend services. It acts as a reverse proxy that handles cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, routing, and observability.
Core value: decouples clients from backend service topology; centralizes cross-cutting concerns; enables independent service evolution; simplifies client development.

list.item: Routing — direct requests to appropriate backend services (critical)
Authentication — validate tokens, API keys, mTLS (critical)
Authorization — enforce access policies (critical)
Rate Limiting — prevent abuse, protect backends (high)
Load Balancing — distribute traffic across instances (high)
Circuit Breaking — handle backend failures gracefully (high)
Observability — metrics, logging, distributed tracing (high)
Request Transformation — modify headers, body, path (medium)
Response Aggregation — combine multiple backend responses (medium)
Caching — cache responses to reduce backend load (medium)
API Versioning — route to different API versions (medium)

section.name: pattern-simple-routing
section.p: Routes requests to appropriate backend services based on path, host, or headers.

rule.statement: Use simple routing when the architecture has a direct mapping between client requests and backend services with minimal transformation needed and a single client type or uniform client needs.
rule.rationale: Simple routing avoids operational overhead of BFF or aggregation patterns. Adding patterns prematurely creates maintenance burden without benefit.

example.title: Simple routing path mapping
example.code: Client: GET /users/123 → API Gateway → User Service (GET /123) Client: GET /orders/456 → API Gateway → Order Service (GET /456)
example.note: One gateway, direct path-to-service mapping, no transformation.

section.name: pattern-bff
section.p: Backend for Frontend (BFF): separate gateways optimized for each client type.

rule.statement: Use BFF when different clients have vastly different data needs, mobile needs optimized payloads, third-party integrations require different contracts, or different security requirements exist per client type.
rule.rationale: A single gateway serving all clients tends to accumulate client-specific logic and becomes a bottleneck; BFF pushes that complexity to per-client surfaces owned by the teams that know those clients.

example.title: BFF topology
example.code: Mobile App --> Mobile BFF Gateway --> Backend Services Web App --> Web BFF Gateway --> Backend Services Admin --> Admin BFF Gateway --> Backend Services Partner --> Partner API Gateway --> Backend Services

section.name: pattern-aggregation
section.p: Aggregation gateway combines responses from multiple backend services into a single response, reducing client-side complexity and network round trips for composite API views.

rule.statement: Use aggregation when reducing client-side complexity is a primary goal, when minimizing network round trips matters (mobile, low-bandwidth), or when creating composite API views from independently deployed services.
rule.rationale: Without aggregation, clients make N parallel requests; mobile clients on slow links feel latency multiplied by the number of services. Aggregation at the gateway trades gateway complexity for client simplicity.

example.title: Aggregation pattern
example.code: Client: GET /dashboard | API Gateway --- User Svc --- Order Svc --> Aggregated Response --- Stats Svc

section.name: pattern-edge-plus-mesh
section.p: Edge Gateway with Service Mesh: gateway handles north-south traffic; service mesh handles east-west. External traffic hits an edge gateway (Kong/Envoy); internal service-to-service uses the mesh (Istio/Linkerd) for mTLS, fine-grained traffic management, and zero-trust security.

rule.statement: Use edge+mesh when services require mutual TLS between them, when fine-grained traffic management between services is needed, or when a zero-trust security model is required. Do NOT use a service mesh for a simple two-tier app — the operational overhead is substantial.
rule.rationale: The mesh handles east-west (service-to-service) while the gateway handles north-south (client-to-gateway). Combining them avoids duplicating concerns at both layers.

list.item: Kong (OSS/Enterprise) — multi-cloud, plugin extensibility, ~50k TPS/node, 100+ plugins. Best: multi-cloud + plugin needs.
AWS API Gateway — managed, AWS-native, serverless, auto-scaling. Best: AWS-native + serverless.
Traefik — OSS, Kubernetes-native, GitOps, ~30k QPS, middleware plugins. Best: K8s + GitOps workflow.
Envoy — OSS, service mesh, high performance, ~35k QPS, WASM extensible. Best: service mesh architecture.
APISIX — OSS, high performance, dashboard, ~50k+ QPS, 80+ plugins. Best: maximum throughput.
Apigee — enterprise, API management, monetization, auto-scaling. Best: enterprise + monetization.
Tyk — OSS/enterprise, GraphQL support, analytics, OpenAPI native. Best: GraphQL + analytics.
Apollo Router — OSS/SaaS, purpose-built GraphQL federation, Rust-based. Best: GraphQL federation.
Need managed service + AWS ecosystem? → AWS API Gateway
Need extensive plugin ecosystem + multi-cloud? → Kong or APISIX
Kubernetes-native with GitOps workflow? → Traefik
Building service mesh architecture? → Envoy (with Istio/Solo)
Need GraphQL federation? → Apollo Router or Tyk
Enterprise API management with monetization? → Apigee or Kong Enterprise

section.name: agent-agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive design with sonnet (pick gateway product and patterns), generate config with haiku from templates, then opus reviews for security and anti-patterns. Always pair the gateway agent with the security and observability methodology folders in context — auth and tracing are inseparable from gateway design.
Output required: gateway config (Kong YAML / CFN / xDS), OpenAPI doc, OTEL pipeline, and load-test script. Reject diffs without all four.

checklist.check: Estimate expected requests per second (RPS) and peak traffic patterns
Identify burst capacity requirements and payload size distribution
List all client types (web, mobile, IoT, partners) and their authentication methods
Document payload format preferences (JSON, Protocol Buffers) per client
Inventory all backend services: endpoints, protocols (REST, gRPC, GraphQL), health check endpoints
Define latency SLA (p50, p95, p99) and availability target (99.9%, 99.99%)
Evaluate Simple Routing vs BFF vs Aggregation patterns against client diversity
Assess need for multiple gateways (regional, client-specific)
Determine if service mesh integration is needed for east-west traffic
Compare gateway solutions against requirements (open-source, managed, plugin ecosystem, K8s integration, vendor lock-in, cost at scale)
Decide on deployment environment (Kubernetes, ECS, VMs) and HA configuration
Plan blue-green/canary deployment approach for gateway itself

antipattern.description: Business logic in gateway plugins (Lua, Wasm, Lambda authorizers).
antipattern.reason: Gateway becomes a deployment bottleneck and an invisible monolith; debt accumulates silently. Keep gateway thin — routing, auth, rate-limit, observability only. Push domain logic to services.

antipattern.description: Gateway as orchestrator — using it to call multiple services sequentially and assemble responses with conditional branching.
antipattern.reason: Tight coupling and bottleneck; use choreography or a dedicated orchestrator (Temporal, Step Functions) instead.

antipattern.description: Single gateway as a single point of failure.
antipattern.reason: Regional gateways and high availability are required. A single gateway instance fails the entire platform.

antipattern.description: Wildcard upstreams — agents emit paths: ["/"] catch-all routes.
antipattern.reason: Explicitly enumerate paths; catch-alls route unexpected traffic to wrong backends and make security audits impossible.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
I need to design an API gateway architecture for the following system:
Context: - System type: [e-commerce / SaaS / B2B API / internal microservices] - Expected traffic: [X RPS peak, Y average] - Client types: [web, mobile, IoT, partner APIs] - Backend services: [list services with protocols] - Current infrastructure: [Kubernetes / AWS / GCP / on-premise]
Requirements: - Availability target: [99.9% / 99.99%] - Latency SLA: p95 < [X]ms - Security: [public API / internal only / mixed] - Compliance: [GDPR / HIPAA / PCI-DSS / none]
Please provide: 1. Recommended gateway pattern (simple routing / BFF / aggregation) 2. Technology recommendation with reasoning 3. High-level architecture description 4. Key configuration considerations 5. Potential risks and mitigations

Help me choose between API gateway solutions for my use case:
Requirements: - Deployment: [Kubernetes / AWS / GCP / Docker] - Scale: [X RPS, Y services] - Features needed: [list required features] - Team expertise: [languages/tools familiar with] - Budget: [open source only / enterprise budget / managed OK]
Compare: Kong, AWS API Gateway, Traefik, Envoy, APISIX.
Provide a comparison matrix and final recommendation with reasoning.

I need to migrate from [current gateway] to [target gateway].
Current State: - Gateway: [current solution] - Routes: [X routes] - Plugins/features in use: [list] - Traffic: [X RPS] - Pain points: [why migrating]
Target State: - Gateway: [target solution] - Additional requirements: [new features needed]
Please provide: 1. Migration strategy (big bang vs incremental) 2. Feature mapping from old to new 3. Risk assessment 4. Rollback plan 5. Estimated complexity (high/medium/low)
